Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis

Analyzing Vacant and Abandoned Buildings Reported in Chicago

AUTHOR

Veronica Zheng

PUBLISHED

April 7, 2026

Introduction

In this lab, I use vacant and abandoned building reports as the predictor variable in a spatial predictive analysis of burglary risk in Chicago.

According to the City of Chicago data portal, this dataset contains historical 311 calls for open and vacant buildings reported since January 1, 2010. It includes the service request date, address information, and geographic coordinates, which makes it appropriate for spatial analysis.

I chose this 311 violation type because vacant and abandoned buildings are often associated with neighborhood disinvestment and disorder, which may be related to burglary patterns across space.

Part 1: Data Loading and Exploration

In this step, I loaded the vacant and abandoned building 311 data, Chicago boundary data, police district boundaries, and burglary data. I also cleaned the vacancy dataset by fixing the date field, removing missing coordinates, and converting the data into spatial points. This step is important because spatial analysis depends on accurate locations, and exploring the data first helps me understand whether vacancy reports are randomly distributed or spatially concentrated. From the summary tables and maps, I found that vacancy reports are unevenly distributed across Chicago, with higher concentrations in some parts of the South and Northwest sides, which suggests clear spatial clustering.

1. Load vacancy and boundary datasets

Code 
# Load police districts (used later for spatial cross-validation)
police_districts <- st_read(
  "https://data.cityofchicago.org/api/geospatial/24zt-jpfn?method=export&format=GeoJSON",
  quiet = TRUE
) |>
  clean_names() |>
  st_transform("ESRI:102271")

# Load Chicago city boundary
chicago_boundary <- st_read(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/urbanSpatial/Public-Policy-Analytics-Landing/master/DATA/Chapter5/chicagoBoundary.geojson",
  quiet = TRUE
) |>
  st_transform("ESRI:102271")

# Load vacancy 311 data
vacant_raw <- read_csv(
  here("data/Vacant_and_Abandoned_Buildings_Reported.csv")
) |>
  clean_names()

burglaries <- st_read(here("data", "burglaries.shp")) %>% 
  st_transform('ESRI:102271')
Reading layer `burglaries' from data source 
  `C:\Users\muyan\Documents\UPenn Files\CPLN 5920\CPLN-5920-Portfolio\data\burglaries.shp' 
  using driver `ESRI Shapefile'
Simple feature collection with 7482 features and 22 fields
Geometry type: POINT
Dimension:     XY
Bounding box:  xmin: 340492 ymin: 552959.6 xmax: 367153.5 ymax: 594815.1
Projected CRS: NAD83(HARN) / Illinois East

2. Clean 311 Data

Code 
vacant_sf <- vacant_raw |>
  mutate(
    date_service_request_was_received = mdy(date_service_request_was_received)
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(longitude), !is.na(latitude)) |>
  filter(
    longitude > -88.0, longitude < -87.5,
    latitude  > 41.6, latitude  < 42.1
  ) |>
  st_as_sf(
    coords = c("longitude", "latitude"),
    crs = 4326,
    remove = FALSE
  ) |>
  st_transform("ESRI:102271")

glimpse(vacant_sf)
Rows: 65,072
Columns: 24
$ service_request_type                                              <chr> "Vac…
$ service_request_number                                            <chr> "18-…
$ date_service_request_was_received                                 <date> 201…
$ location_of_building_on_the_lot_if_garage_change_type_code_to_bgd <chr> "Fro…
$ is_the_building_dangerous_or_hazardous                            <lgl> NA, …
$ is_building_open_or_boarded                                       <chr> "Ope…
$ if_the_building_is_open_where_is_the_entry_point                  <chr> "BAC…
$ is_the_building_currently_vacant_or_occupied                      <chr> "Vac…

$ any_people_using_property_homeless_childen_gangs                  <lgl> TRUE…

$ geometry                                                          <POINT [m]> …

3. Basic summaries

Basic information:

Code 
basic_summary <- tibble(
  metric = c(
    "Total number of reports",
    "Earliest report date",
    "Latest report date"
  ),
  value = c(
    nrow(vacant_sf),
    as.character(min(vacant_sf$date_service_request_was_received, na.rm = TRUE)),
    as.character(max(vacant_sf$date_service_request_was_received, na.rm = TRUE))
  )
)

kable(
  basic_summary,
  caption = "Basic Summary of Vacant and Abandoned Building Reports"
)
Basic Summary of Vacant and Abandoned Building Reports
metric value
Total number of reports 65072
Earliest report date 2008-04-03
Latest report date 2018-12-17

Data by year:

Code 
vacant_sf |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  mutate(year = year(date_service_request_was_received)) |>
  count(year) |>
  kable(caption = "Number of Reports by Year")
Number of Reports by Year
year n
2008 33
2009 434
2010 6186
2011 15380
2012 11976
2013 8366
2014 5450
2015 5314
2016 4434
2017 3653
2018 3846

The number of reports peaked in 2011 and 2012, which shows an uneven distribution across time.

Visualizations:

Code 
# Create plotting versions in lon/lat so both maps display cleanly
boundary_plot <- st_transform(chicago_boundary, 4326)
vacant_plot   <- st_transform(vacant_sf, 4326)

vacant_coords <- st_coordinates(vacant_plot) |>
  as.data.frame()

map1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = boundary_plot, fill = "gray95", color = "gray60", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = vacant_plot, color = "red", alpha = 0.35, size = 0.15) +
  labs(
    title = "Point Distribution of 311 Reports",
    subtitle = "Vacant and abandoned building reports with valid coordinates"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

map2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = boundary_plot, fill = "gray95", color = "gray60", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_density_2d_filled(
    data = vacant_coords,
    aes(X, Y),
    alpha = 0.7,
    bins = 8
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Density Surface of Vacancy Reports",
    x = "Longitude",
    y = "Latitude"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

map1 + map2

Interpretation of distribution:

The two maps show that vacant and abandoned building reports are not evenly distributed across Chicago. A few community areas have noticeably higher numbers of reports than the rest of the city, especially in parts of the central South and Northwest sides. In contrast, many northern and eastern community areas have relatively low counts. his pattern suggests that vacant and abandoned building complaints are associated with uneven neighborhood conditions across Chicago.

Part 2: Fishnet Grid Creation

In this step, I created a 500 by 500 fishnet grid and counted how many vacancy reports fell inside each grid cell. This is important because the fishnet gives me a consistent unit of analysis across the city, which makes it easier to compare places and build count models. After aggregation, I found that many grid cells had very low counts while a smaller number of cells had much higher counts.

1. Create a 500 by 500 fishnet

Code 
# Create fishnet based on Chicago boundary
fishnet <- st_make_grid(
  chicago_boundary,
  cellsize = 500,
  square = TRUE
) |>
  st_as_sf() |>
  mutate(grid_id = row_number())

# Keep only cells that intersect Chicago
fishnet <- fishnet[chicago_boundary, ]

2. Aggregate 311 to grid cells

Code 
vacant_grid_counts <- st_join(
  vacant_sf,
  fishnet,
  join = st_within,
  left = FALSE
) |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  count(grid_id, name = "report_count")

fishnet_counts <- fishnet |>
  left_join(vacant_grid_counts, by = "grid_id") |>
  mutate(report_count = replace_na(report_count, 0))

Summarize report counts:

Code 
grid_summary <- tibble(
  metric = c(
    "Number of grid cells",
    "Grid cells with at least one report",
    "Maximum reports in a grid cell",
    "Mean reports per grid cell"
  ),
  value = c(
    nrow(fishnet_counts),
    sum(fishnet_counts$report_count > 0),
    max(fishnet_counts$report_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    round(mean(fishnet_counts$report_count, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
  )
)

kable(
  grid_summary,
  caption = "Summary Statistics of Vacant and Abandoned Building Reports by Fishnet Cell"
)
Summary Statistics of Vacant and Abandoned Building Reports by Fishnet Cell
metric value
Number of grid cells 2458.00
Grid cells with at least one report 1916.00
Maximum reports in a grid cell 378.00
Mean reports per grid cell 26.47

3. Visualize distribution

Code 
ggplot(fishnet_counts) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = report_count), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#eff3ff",
    high = "#084594"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Vacant and Abandoned Building Reports by Fishnet Cell",
    subtitle = "Aggregated to regular grid cells across Chicago",
    fill = "Reports"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

Interpretation:

This map shows the spatial distribution that is aggregated by fishnet cells. It shows the same pattern as the previous maps.

Part 3. Spatial Feaatures

In this step, I created spatial features that describe the relationship between each grid cell and nearby cells. I calculated the nearest-neighbor vacancy count, used Local Moran’s I to identify hot spots and cold spots, and measured the distance from each cell to the nearest hot spot. This step is important because spatial patterns are influenced not only by what happens in one cell, but also by what happens around it. The results showed that vacancy reports form significant hot spots in some parts of Chicago, while most cells were not statistically significant. This means strong clustering exists in selected areas, and proximity to these clusters may help explain burglary patterns.

1. K-nearest neighbour features

The k-nearest neighbor method is used to define nearby grid cells and create spatial features based on neighboring report counts.

Code 
# Add police district ID to grid cells for later cross-validation
fishnet_counts <- st_join(fishnet_counts, police_districts, left = TRUE)

# Create centroids for nearest-neighbor calculations
fishnet_centroids <- st_centroid(fishnet_counts)
coords <- st_coordinates(fishnet_centroids)

# Define 5 nearest neighbors
knn_5 <- knearneigh(coords, k = 3)
nb_5 <- knn2nb(knn_5)

# Create row-standardized spatial weights
lw_5 <- nb2listw(nb_5, style = "W", zero.policy = TRUE)

# Neighbor vacancy count (same idea as teacher's abandoned_cars.nn)
fishnet_counts$report_count_nn <- lag.listw(
  lw_5,
  fishnet_counts$report_count,
  zero.policy = TRUE
)

knitr::kable(
  fishnet_counts |>
    st_drop_geometry() |>
    summarise(
      mean_report_count = mean(report_count, na.rm = TRUE),
      mean_report_count_nn = mean(report_count_nn, na.rm = TRUE)
    ),
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Summary of Vacancy Count and Neighbor Vacancy Count"
)
Summary of Vacancy Count and Neighbor Vacancy Count
mean_report_count mean_report_count_nn
26.787 26.699

2. Local Moran’s I with Hot Spot, Cold Spot

Local Moran’s I then uses this neighborhood structure to identify statistically significant hot spots and cold spots.

Code 
local_moran <- localmoran(
  fishnet_counts$report_count,
  lw_5,
  zero.policy = TRUE
)

fishnet_counts <- fishnet_counts |>
  mutate(
    local_i = local_moran[, 1],
    local_i_z = local_moran[, 4],
    local_i_p = local_moran[, 5]
  )
#spatial lag
fishnet_counts$lag_count <- lag.listw(
  lw_5,
  fishnet_counts$report_count,
  zero.policy = TRUE
)
#hot/cold spot
count_mean <- mean(fishnet_counts$report_count, na.rm = TRUE)

fishnet_counts$cluster_type <- "Not Significant"

fishnet_counts$cluster_type[
  fishnet_counts$local_i_p <= 0.05 &
  fishnet_counts$report_count >= count_mean &
  fishnet_counts$lag_count >= count_mean
] <- "High-High"

fishnet_counts$cluster_type[
  fishnet_counts$local_i_p <= 0.05 &
  fishnet_counts$report_count < count_mean &
  fishnet_counts$lag_count < count_mean
] <- "Low-Low"

fishnet_counts$cluster_type[
  fishnet_counts$local_i_p <= 0.05 &
  fishnet_counts$report_count >= count_mean &
  fishnet_counts$lag_count < count_mean
] <- "High-Low"

fishnet_counts$cluster_type[
  fishnet_counts$local_i_p <= 0.05 &
  fishnet_counts$report_count < count_mean &
  fishnet_counts$lag_count >= count_mean
] <- "Low-High"

#result table
fishnet_counts |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  count(cluster_type) |>
  kable(caption = "Local Moran's I Cluster Classification")
Local Moran’s I Cluster Classification
cluster_type n
High-High 283
Low-High 10
Not Significant 2601

Local Moran’s I Visualization:

Code 
ggplot(fishnet_counts) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = cluster_type), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "High-High" = "#b2182b",
      "Low-Low" = "#2166ac",
      "High-Low" = "#ef8a62",
      "Low-High" = "#67a9cf",
      "Not Significant" = "grey85"
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Local Moran's I Cluster Map",
    fill = "Cluster Type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

Interpretation:

The Local Moran’s I cluster map shows that statistically significant hot spots of vacant and abandoned building reports are concentrated in central south and northwest Chicago.

Most grid cells are classified as not significant, which means that many parts of the city do not show strong local clustering. In addition, a small number of Low-High cells appear near hotspot areas. These cells have relatively low report counts themselves, but they are surrounded by neighbors with higher counts.

3. Distance-to-hotspot measures

Code 
# Keep only hotspot cells
hotspots <- fishnet_counts |>
  filter(cluster_type == "High-High")

# Create centroids for all cells and hotspot cells
all_centroids <- st_centroid(fishnet_counts)
hotspot_centroids <- st_centroid(hotspots)

# Calculate distance to nearest hotspot
if (nrow(hotspots) > 0) {
  dist_matrix <- st_distance(all_centroids, hotspot_centroids)
  fishnet_counts$dist_to_hotspot <- apply(dist_matrix, 1, min) |> as.numeric()
} else {
  fishnet_counts$dist_to_hotspot <- NA_real_
}
Code 
#summary table
distance_summary <- tibble(
  metric = c(
    "Minimum distance to hotspot",
    "Maximum distance to hotspot",
    "Mean distance to hotspot"
  ),
  value = c(
    round(min(fishnet_counts$dist_to_hotspot, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    round(max(fishnet_counts$dist_to_hotspot, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    round(mean(fishnet_counts$dist_to_hotspot, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
  )
)

kable(
  distance_summary,
  caption = "Summary of Distance to Nearest Hot Spot"
)
Summary of Distance to Nearest Hot Spot
metric value
Minimum distance to hotspot 0.00
Maximum distance to hotspot 14115.59
Mean distance to hotspot 3791.57
Code 
ggplot(fishnet_counts) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = dist_to_hotspot), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#eff3ff",
    high = "#084594"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distance to Nearest Hot Spot",
    fill = "Distance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

Part 4: Count Regression Models

In this step, I used the vacancy-related features to model burglary counts with Poisson and Negative Binomial regression. This step is important because burglary counts are count data, so I need a model that matches that type of outcome. I first tested the Poisson model, but the overdispersion check suggested that the data were too variable for Poisson. The Negative Binomial model had a lower AIC, which means it fit the data better. This shows that the burglary counts are overdispersed and that the Negative Binomial model is more appropriate for this analysis.

Data Preparation:

Code 
# Aggregate burglaries to the same fishnet grid
burglary_grid_counts <- st_join(
  burglaries,
  fishnet,
  join = st_within,
  left = FALSE
) |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  count(grid_id, name = "burglary_count")

# Join burglary counts to the vacancy feature dataset
model_sf <- fishnet_counts |>
  left_join(burglary_grid_counts, by = "grid_id") |>
  mutate(burglary_count = replace_na(burglary_count, 0))

model_data <- model_sf |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  dplyr::select(
    burglary_count,
    report_count,
    report_count_nn,
    dist_to_hotspot
  ) |>
  na.omit() |>
  as.data.frame()

knitr::kable(
  model_data |>
    summarise(
      observations = n(),
      mean_burglary = mean(burglary_count),
      var_burglary = var(burglary_count)
    ),
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Summary of Modeling Data"
)
Summary of Modeling Data
observations mean_burglary var_burglary
2894 3.053 13.467

1. Fit Poisson regression

Code 
pois_model <- glmmTMB(
  burglary_count ~ report_count + report_count_nn + dist_to_hotspot,
  family = poisson,
  data = model_data
)

pois_results <- broom.mixed::tidy(
  pois_model,
  conf.int = TRUE,
  exponentiate = TRUE
)

knitr::kable(
  pois_results,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Poisson Regression Results for Burglary Counts"
)
Poisson Regression Results for Burglary Counts
effect component term estimate std.error statistic p.value conf.low conf.high
fixed cond (Intercept) 3.161 0.075 48.399 0.000 3.017 3.312
fixed cond report_count 1.005 0.000 15.669 0.000 1.004 1.006
fixed cond report_count_nn 1.001 0.000 2.610 0.009 1.000 1.002
fixed cond dist_to_hotspot 1.000 0.000 -17.390 0.000 1.000 1.000

overdispersion check

Code 
pearson_resid <- residuals(pois_model, type = "pearson")
n_obs <- nrow(model_data)
n_par <- length(fixef(pois_model)$cond)

dispersion_ratio <- sum(pearson_resid^2) / (n_obs - n_par)

overdisp_table <- tibble(
  Metric = c("Dispersion ratio", "Rule of thumb"),
  Value = c(round(dispersion_ratio, 3), "> 1.5 suggests overdispersion")
)

knitr::kable(
  overdisp_table,
  caption = "Poisson Overdispersion Check"
)
Poisson Overdispersion Check
Metric Value
Dispersion ratio 3.343
Rule of thumb > 1.5 suggests overdispersion

This suggests that Poisson has overdispersion, so its worth consider other regression.

2. Fit Negative Binomaial regression

Code 
nb_model <- glmmTMB(
  burglary_count ~ report_count + report_count_nn + dist_to_hotspot,
  family = nbinom2,
  data = model_data
)

nb_results <- broom.mixed::tidy(
  nb_model,
  conf.int = TRUE,
  exponentiate = TRUE
)

knitr::kable(
  nb_results,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Negative Binomial Regression Results for Burglary Counts"
)
Negative Binomial Regression Results for Burglary Counts
effect component term estimate std.error statistic p.value conf.low conf.high
fixed cond (Intercept) 2.618 0.132 19.064 0.000 2.371 2.890
fixed cond report_count 1.008 0.001 7.863 0.000 1.006 1.010
fixed cond report_count_nn 1.001 0.001 0.871 0.384 0.999 1.003
fixed cond dist_to_hotspot 1.000 0.000 -7.112 0.000 1.000 1.000

3. AIC Comparison

Code 
model_compare <- tibble(
  Model = c("Poisson", "Negative Binomial"),
  AIC = c(AIC(pois_model), AIC(nb_model))
)

knitr::kable(
  model_compare,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "AIC Comparison of Count Models"
)
AIC Comparison of Count Models
Model AIC
Poisson 15290.93
Negative Binomial 12470.05

Interpretation:

Lower AIC values indicate better model fit, this suggests that the Negative Binomial model fits the report count data much better than the Poisson model.

Part 5: Spatial Cross-Validation (2017)

In this step, I used leave-one-group-out spatial cross-validation based on police districts for the 2017 data. I trained the model on all districts except one and then tested it on the district that was left out, repeating this process for each group. This step is important because it tests whether the model can predict burglary counts in new areas rather than only fitting the same locations used for training. The results showed moderate prediction error, which means the vacancy-based features do have predictive value, but they are not enough to explain burglary patterns perfectly across all districts.

1. Prepare 2017 Data

Code 
# 2017 vacancy reports
vacant_2017 <- vacant_sf |>
  filter(year(date_service_request_was_received) == 2017)

# Aggregate 2017 vacancy reports to fishnet
vacant_2017_grid_counts <- st_join(
  vacant_2017,
  fishnet,
  join = st_within,
  left = FALSE
) |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  count(grid_id, name = "report_count")

fishnet_2017 <- fishnet |>
  left_join(vacant_2017_grid_counts, by = "grid_id") |>
  mutate(report_count = replace_na(report_count, 0))

2. Spatial Feature of 2017 Data

Code 
# Add police district
fishnet_2017 <- st_join(fishnet_2017, police_districts, left = TRUE)

# Create centroids
fishnet_2017_centroids <- st_centroid(fishnet_2017)
coords_2017 <- st_coordinates(fishnet_2017_centroids)

# 5 nearest neighbors
knn_2017 <- knearneigh(coords_2017, k = 5)
nb_2017 <- knn2nb(knn_2017)
lw_2017 <- nb2listw(nb_2017, style = "W", zero.policy = TRUE)

# Neighbor vacancy count
fishnet_2017$report_count_nn <- lag.listw(
  lw_2017,
  fishnet_2017$report_count,
  zero.policy = TRUE
)

3. Recalculate vacancy hot spots and distance to hot spots

Code 
local_moran_2017 <- localmoran(
  fishnet_2017$report_count,
  lw_2017,
  zero.policy = TRUE
)

fishnet_2017 <- fishnet_2017 |>
  mutate(
    local_i = local_moran_2017[, 1],
    local_i_z = local_moran_2017[, 4],
    local_i_p = local_moran_2017[, 5]
  )

fishnet_2017$lag_count <- lag.listw(
  lw_2017,
  fishnet_2017$report_count,
  zero.policy = TRUE
)

count_mean_2017 <- mean(fishnet_2017$report_count, na.rm = TRUE)

fishnet_2017$cluster_type <- "Not Significant"

fishnet_2017$cluster_type[
  fishnet_2017$local_i_p <= 0.05 &
    fishnet_2017$report_count >= count_mean_2017 &
    fishnet_2017$lag_count >= count_mean_2017
] <- "High-High"

fishnet_2017$cluster_type[
  fishnet_2017$local_i_p <= 0.05 &
    fishnet_2017$report_count < count_mean_2017 &
    fishnet_2017$lag_count < count_mean_2017
] <- "Low-Low"

fishnet_2017$cluster_type[
  fishnet_2017$local_i_p <= 0.05 &
    fishnet_2017$report_count >= count_mean_2017 &
    fishnet_2017$lag_count < count_mean_2017
] <- "High-Low"

fishnet_2017$cluster_type[
  fishnet_2017$local_i_p <= 0.05 &
    fishnet_2017$report_count < count_mean_2017 &
    fishnet_2017$lag_count >= count_mean_2017
] <- "Low-High"


hotspots_2017 <- fishnet_2017 |>
  filter(cluster_type == "High-High")

all_centroids_2017 <- st_centroid(fishnet_2017)

if (nrow(hotspots_2017) > 0) {
  hotspot_centroids_2017 <- st_centroid(hotspots_2017)
  dist_matrix_2017 <- st_distance(all_centroids_2017, hotspot_centroids_2017)
  fishnet_2017$dist_to_hotspot <- apply(dist_matrix_2017, 1, min) |> as.numeric()
} else {
  fishnet_2017$dist_to_hotspot <- NA_real_
}

4. Aggregate burglary counts to the same fishnet

Code 
# Keep only 2017 burglaries
burglaries_2017 <- burglaries |>
  filter(Year == 2017)

# Aggregate 2017 burglaries to the fishnet
burglary_2017_grid_counts <- st_join(
  burglaries_2017,
  fishnet,
  join = st_within,
  left = FALSE
) |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  count(grid_id, name = "burglary_count")

# Join 2017 burglary counts to the 2017 fishnet data
fishnet_2017 <- fishnet_2017 |>
  left_join(burglary_2017_grid_counts, by = "grid_id") |>
  mutate(burglary_count = replace_na(burglary_count, 0))

5. Prepare cv group

Code 
fishnet_2017_cv <- fishnet_2017 |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  dplyr::select(
    grid_id,
    burglary_count,
    report_count,
    report_count_nn,
    dist_to_hotspot,
    dist_num
  ) |>
  na.omit()

group_ids <- fishnet_2017_cv |>
  distinct(dist_num) |>
  drop_na() |>
  pull(dist_num)

knitr::kable(
  fishnet_2017_cv |>
    count(dist_num),
  caption = "Number of Grid Cells in Each Police District Group"
)
Number of Grid Cells in Each Police District Group
dist_num n
1 71
10 111
11 79
12 127
14 84
15 60
16 224
17 126
18 74
19 116
2 99
20 61
22 187
24 76
25 135
3 86
31 37
4 325
5 170
6 109
7 86
8 281
9 167

6. Run Leave-One-Group-Out cross-validation

Code 
cv_results <- purrr::map_dfr(group_ids, function(g) {
  
  train_data <- fishnet_2017_cv |>
    filter(dist_num != g)
  
  test_data <- fishnet_2017_cv |>
    filter(dist_num == g)
  
  if (nrow(train_data) == 0 || nrow(test_data) == 0) {
    return(NULL)
  }
  
  cv_model <- glmmTMB(
    burglary_count ~ report_count + report_count_nn + dist_to_hotspot,
    family = nbinom2,
    data = train_data
  )
  
  tibble(
    group = g,
    observed = test_data$burglary_count,
    predicted = predict(cv_model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")
  )
})

7. Calculate MAE and RMSE

Code 
cv_metrics <- cv_results |>
  summarise(
    MAE = mean(abs(observed - predicted), na.rm = TRUE),
    RMSE = sqrt(mean((observed - predicted)^2, na.rm = TRUE))
  )

cat("Spatial Cross-Validation Results\n")
Spatial Cross-Validation Results
Code 
cat("MAE :", round(cv_metrics$MAE, 3), "\n")
MAE : 2.586
Code 
cat("RMSE:", round(cv_metrics$RMSE, 3), "\n")
RMSE: 3.661

Interpretation:

I used leave-one-group-out spatial cross-validation to evaluate whether vacancy-based spatial features can predict burglary counts in police districts that were not used to train the model. The cross-validation results suggest that the model performs reasonably but not perfectly when predicting burglary counts across space. The MAE of 2.565 indicates moderate average prediction error, while the RMSE of 3.519 shows that larger errors occur in some locations. This means that vacancy-related spatial features are useful predictors of burglary, but additional factors would likely be needed to improve model accuracy.

Part 6: Model Evaluation

In this step, I compared the vacancy-based Negative Binomial model with a KDE baseline. I created KDE predictions from the burglary point pattern and then compared both approaches using maps, MAE, and RMSE. This step is important because it helps me see whether the more complex regression model performs better than a simple spatial baseline. The results showed that the KDE baseline performed better overall, which suggests that the general spatial concentration of burglaries was captured more effectively by KDE than by the vacancy-based model. This means vacancy reports are related to burglary risk, but they do not explain the full spatial pattern on their own.

1. KDE

Code 
# Make sure burglaries and boundary use the same CRS
burglaries <- st_transform(burglaries, st_crs(chicago_boundary))

# Create ppp object for KDE
burg_coords <- st_coordinates(burglaries)
city_window <- as.owin(st_bbox(chicago_boundary))

burg_ppp <- ppp(
  x = burg_coords[, 1],
  y = burg_coords[, 2],
  window = city_window
)

# Kernel density estimate
burg_kde <- density.ppp(
  burg_ppp,
  sigma = 1000,
  edge = TRUE
)

2. Convert KDE to raster and extract values to fishnet

Code 
# Convert KDE to terra raster
kde_raster <- rast(burg_kde)

# Extract mean KDE value to each fishnet cell
fishnet_kde <- fishnet |>
  mutate(
    kde_value = terra::extract(
      kde_raster,
      vect(fishnet),
      fun = mean,
      na.rm = TRUE
    )[, 2]
  )

3. Put actual burglary counts and model predictors into one fishnet object

Code 
fishnet_model <- fishnet_counts |>
  left_join(burglary_grid_counts, by = "grid_id") |>
  mutate(burglary_count = replace_na(burglary_count, 0)) |>
  filter(
    !is.na(report_count),
    !is.na(report_count_nn),
    !is.na(dist_to_hotspot)
  )

###4. Fit final vacancy-based model and create predictions

Code 
fishnet_model$prediction_nb <- predict(
  nb_model,
  newdata = st_drop_geometry(fishnet_model),
  type = "response"
)

5. Convert KDE to predicted burglary counts

Code 
fishnet_model <- fishnet_model |>
  left_join(
    fishnet_kde |>
      st_drop_geometry() |>
      dplyr::select(grid_id, kde_value),
    by = "grid_id"
  )

kde_sum <- sum(fishnet_model$kde_value, na.rm = TRUE)
count_sum <- sum(fishnet_model$burglary_count, na.rm = TRUE)

fishnet_model <- fishnet_model |>
  mutate(
    prediction_kde = (kde_value / kde_sum) * count_sum
  )

6. Compare actual counts, NB predictions, and KDE predictions

Code 
p1 <- ggplot(fishnet_model) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = burglary_count), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "Actual Burglaries", fill = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(fishnet_model) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = prediction_nb), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "Negative Binomial Prediction", fill = "Predicted") +
  theme_minimal()

p3 <- ggplot(fishnet_model) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = prediction_kde), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "KDE Baseline Prediction", fill = "Predicted") +
  theme_minimal()

p1 + p2 + p3

Interpretation:

The Negative Binomial model captures some higher-risk areas, but its predictions appear more uneven and include stronger overprediction in a small number of cells, while the KDE baseline more closely follows the broader spatial concentration of burglaries across Chicago.

7. Compare model performance with KDE baseline

Code 
comparison_table <- tibble(
  Model = c("Vacancy-Based Negative Binomial Model", "KDE Baseline"),
  MAE = c(
    mean(abs(fishnet_model$burglary_count - fishnet_model$prediction_nb), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(fishnet_model$burglary_count - fishnet_model$prediction_kde), na.rm = TRUE)
  ),
  RMSE = c(
    sqrt(mean((fishnet_model$burglary_count - fishnet_model$prediction_nb)^2, na.rm = TRUE)),
    sqrt(mean((fishnet_model$burglary_count - fishnet_model$prediction_kde)^2, na.rm = TRUE))
  )
)

knitr::kable(
  comparison_table,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Comparison of Vacancy-Based Model and KDE Baseline"
)
Comparison of Vacancy-Based Model and KDE Baseline
Model MAE RMSE
Vacancy-Based Negative Binomial Model 2.507 3.878
KDE Baseline 1.954 2.831

Interpretation:

The model comparison shows that the KDE baseline outperformed the vacancy-based Negative Binomial model.This means that the KDE approach predicted burglary counts more accurately overall and also had fewer large prediction errors.

8. Error Analysis

Code 
fishnet_model <- fishnet_model |>
  mutate(
    residual_nb = burglary_count - prediction_nb,
    residual_kde = burglary_count - prediction_kde,
    abs_error_nb = abs(burglary_count - prediction_nb)
  )

ggplot(fishnet_model) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = residual_nb), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#2166ac",
    mid = "white",
    high = "#b2182b",
    midpoint = 0
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "NB Model Residuals",
    fill = "Residual"
  ) +
  coord_sf(datum = NA) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9)
  )
Code 
ggplot(fishnet_model) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = residual_kde), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#2166ac",
    mid = "white",
    high = "#b2182b",
    midpoint = 0
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "KDE Residuals",
    fill = "Residual"
  ) +
  coord_sf(datum = NA) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9)
  )

Interpretation:

The residual maps show that both models still make spatially uneven prediction errors. In the NB residual map, most cells are close to zero, but there are still some blue and red clusters. This means the Negative Binomial model fits many areas reasonably well, but it still overpredicts in some places and underpredicts in others. The KDE residual map is more dominated by positive values, which suggests that KDE tends to underpredict burglary counts in many cells.
